author: @Elrog3
text: Thermal paste can vary in quality. What brand thermal paste did you compare against? Also, its important to take note of the ambient temperature when doing these tests.

author: @filiplaskovski9993
text: This is what I was looking for

author: @HWandW
text: One reason that I use thermal paste in my PC repair & sales side businesses is that I don’t have to worry about accidentally using too much and causing damage since it isn’t electrically conductive. Alas, usually [electrically] conductive materials are better than the gray goo that I normally use (and usually replace once a year). I should test GD 900 (my “gray goo”) against copper anti seize. I’d like to see a comparison of conductive ink verses silver, graphite/graphene, & diamond based (high quality) thermal pastes. If CPUs, GPUs, north & south bridge chips use copper & aluminum heat sinks then using materials that transfer heat better than copper, then wouldn’t that be overkill? Perhaps materials better than copper in between a processor & heatsink is the way to go since no union of processor & heatsink can be made perfect. Unless the die of the processor is a full size heatsink. You may experience even better results lapping (fine wet sanding) both the cpu die and the heatsink and then applying conductive ink between them.

author: @no1slisteninganyway
text: My laptop does that too using Prime95. It's actively keeping the temperature below 80C by decreasing the clock speed of the CPU. I haven't found a way to deactivate it on my machine because the performance does go down.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: That kinda makes sense, I will have a bit of a search to see if that is a problem that can be turned off. Cheers mate! :)

author: @perstyr
text: You could maybe do something similar with a cheap/old computer - test it with thermal paste, and then conductive ink (whichever is easier to remove first) and do a back to back comparison. Either way, great stuff so far!

author: @ed-jf3xh
text: Do you have any Peltier modules? Would be easier to do a comparison between the ink and thermal compound. A current regulated power supply, 1 Thermal couple to measure with, 1 Peltier module and 2 identical size heat sinks. No fans please. Brilliant!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Awesome idea mate! I never heard of peltier modules before until now. I might have to do this! Cheers mate! :)

replies.author: @ed-jf3xh
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey No worries. Have a great day.

author: @gregx1044
text: My guess on why the CPU temps were dropping is that *different* components were causing the CPU to throttle. I have heard that some laptops will throttle because the power supply voltage regulators overheat. I know your PC isn't a laptop...it's just a guess.

author: @ThisRandomUsername
text:
You should send some conductive ink to someone like Der8auer in Germany. He does some great testing, and really dives quite deep with his methodology, so I'd imagine you'd get quite an unbiased and well tested result.
Your results from the last video on this puts the conductive ink quite close to some of the metal-based compounds if your original thermal paste was any good.

author: @malcomflibbleghast8140
text: why is the ddr3 ram running at 800mhz?  could be running faster....or upgraded with faster sticks.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I never noticed that to be honest lol, both sticks are clocked at 1600mhz. Il get that sorted hahaha. Cheers mate! :)

replies.author: @malcomflibbleghast8140
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey that will ofcourse generate extra heat inside the case......chk if it alters the fsb ?

replies.author: @Elrog3
replies.text: Ram that is marketed at 1600 mhz is actually only 800mhz. They manufacturers use the MT/s (megatransfers per second) and advertise it as mhz. There are 2 megatransfers per cycle, hence the doubled value.

author: @DiyEcoProjects
text:
Wow Luke, thats really great. So the conductive ink hasnt migrated out from under the chip and got into unwelcome places, thats a good thing. Heres a couple more free programs for your tools. Wont let me post urls.

These are useful to manage bloatware on windows 10. Ive used them on -Actions -Use only recommended settings
O&O ShutUp10
O&O AppBuster

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text:
This made me wonder if it would be possible to make a whole heat sink doped with the ink (or just take a regular one and paint the whole thing).  Wonder if it would be more efficient than just the standard metal  one?  

If you had a couple peltier devices lying around, and a couple small heat sinks, you could check how much voltage they produce with the same heat source,  but use thermal paste, just the ink, paint one entirely, and whatever sort you made up- like maybe a doped ceramic?    Dunno.  just a thought I had.

author: @martinrothwell8966
text: Hi, when the CPU temperature droped down that is a sign of it throttling back, going slower,due to it going over heat limit. Benchmark testing does an average. It look like the ink is doing a good job of heat transfer. So many variables, room temperature, how many people in room air movement. Maybe having two identical computer the same running same application at same time would give a better comparison? What do you think?

author: @goldmagnet9013
text: Hi Luke - I don't understand something, most likely I misinterpreted something,  you said that the 3D benchmark results had risen 14 degrees since the first time you ran them, and then said that the result makes sense as the CPU has been working hard this week ? How does using the CPU in the week beforehand make any difference the results now ?

replies.author: @Alexander_Sannikov
replies.text: the ink degraded over the week and reduced its heat transfer

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013

author: @simplisticirony5896
text: Interesting. Have you tried mining bitcoin or high intensity gaming with it? Please keep us posted. Thanks.

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text: Prime95 is pretty much the toughest load you can throw at a CPU, so you'd likely not see temperatures get all that close to his first results no matter what you ran it on. Mining is also typically done on a GPU, and will run a lot slower on a CPU.
